food_name: Fried Crispy Dried Anchovies (Ikan Bilis Goreng)
aliases: 炸小鱼干 (zhà xiǎo yú gān)
Ikan Bilis Goreng Rangup (ikan bilis = dried anchovy; goreng = fried; rangup = crispy)

chinese: 炸小鱼干 (zhà xiǎo yú gān)
malay: Ikan Bilis Goreng Rangup (ikan bilis = dried anchovy; goreng = fried; rangup = crispy)

region_of_origin: Southeast Asia — Singapore and Malaysia; Stolephorus spp. sourced from Tropical Indo-Pacific waters
food_subgroup: Crispy deep-fried whole dried anchovies (Stolephorus spp.); highest concentrated calcium food in Singapore hawker diet; very high sodium; very high purine

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for fried dried anchovy (Stolephorus spp.). Closest parent used: FOODON_03412384 dried anchovy food product. A more specific node for deep-fried preparation is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00TX used as closest parent for dried anchovy. No specific FoodEx2 facet for deep-fried preparation of dried anchovy is available; facet descriptor for frying process should be appended in full FoodEx2 extended term if required.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept exists for fried dried anchovy as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT coverage for Southeast Asian hawker foods is limited.

serving.description: 3 tablespoons as garnish / condiment (~30g fried ikan bilis)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried crispy ikan bilis as a nasi lemak garnish is approximately 3 tablespoons (30g), providing around 89 kcal, 14.4g protein, 2.7g fat, 390mg calcium, and 720mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient Composition of Dried and Fried Anchovies
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for fried dried anchovies (ikan bilis goreng). Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for anchovy (raw and dried) and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. Core macros and micronutrients confirmed from HPB primary source. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values supplemented from USDA SR Legacy (confidence 0.9) where HPB data was not available.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Protein and fat-dominant seafood analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried dried anchovies in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~15 (very low) based on the food's macronutrient profile: ~48g protein/100g, ~9g fat/100g, and only ~3g carbohydrate/100g. This is consistent with GI values for high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood and dried fish products in the Sydney GI database. Glycemic load per 30g serving = (15 × 0.9g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.1–0.5, effectively negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — 2,400mg per 100g; approximately 720mg sodium per standard 30g serving
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit ikan bilis goreng to no more than 10–15g per serving (as a flavour garnish only), as even a 30g nasi lemak portion contributes approximately 720mg sodium — nearly 50% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2,000mg — and larger quantities used as a soup stock base (50–100g) can deliver 1,200–2,400mg sodium into the broth.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content (~3g/100g) and very low glycemic load (~0.5 per 30g serving); high protein supports satiety and glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried crispy ikan bilis is suitable for patients with Type 2 Diabetes as a condiment or garnish: the glycemic load per 30g serving is effectively zero, the high protein content (14.4g per 30g) supports satiety and post-meal glucose stability, and vitamin D (3mcg per 30g, ~20% AI) may support insulin sensitivity — however, sodium intake must be monitored carefully in patients with concurrent hypertension or diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high sodium (2,400mg/100g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; partially offset by high omega-3 fatty acids (~2.8g/100g), high potassium (1,200mg/100g), and high selenium (52mcg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For patients with cardiovascular disease, ikan bilis goreng presents a dual profile: the very high sodium content (720mg per 30g serving) is a significant concern for blood pressure management and fluid retention, but the food is also a concentrated source of cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids and potassium; use should be restricted to small garnish quantities (10–20g) and avoided as a primary stock ingredient in patients with heart failure or poorly controlled hypertension.

gout.key_factor: Very high purine content (~900mg/100g) — absolute contraindication for active gout or hyperuricaemia
gout.guidance: Ikan bilis goreng is absolutely contraindicated in patients with active gout or a history of hyperuricaemia; the purine content (~270mg per 30g serving) far exceeds the recommended daily purine limit for gout patients (~400mg/day total), and even small quantities can precipitate acute gout attacks — substitute with firm tofu for calcium and threadfin or pomfret for omega-3.

osteoporosis.key_factor: Exceptionally high calcium (1,300mg/100g) — highest calcium density of any food in the Singapore hawker diet; also provides vitamin D (10mcg/100g) for calcium absorption
osteoporosis.guidance: Ikan bilis goreng is an outstanding calcium source for patients with osteoporosis or at risk of bone loss: a 30g serving provides approximately 390mg calcium (39% of the 1,000mg daily AI) and 3mcg vitamin D, making it the most calcium-dense food available in the Singapore hawker diet and a valuable dairy-free alternative for lactose-intolerant patients — provided sodium intake is managed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Crispy Dried Anchovies (Ikan Bilis Goreng) are whole small anchovies of the species Stolephorus spp. that have been salt-dried and then deep-fried in neutral oil at 170–180°C for 60–90 seconds until uniformly golden and crispy. The result is a light, intensely savoury, crunchy whole fish that can be eaten bones and all. In Singapore and Malaysia, ikan bilis goreng is the essential garnish of nasi lemak — the national dish — where it provides textural contrast and concentrated umami alongside coconut rice, sambal, cucumber, and hard-boiled egg. It is also used as a flavour base for soups and broths, as a standalone snack, and as a protein-rich topping for congee and rice dishes. Nutritionally, ikan bilis goreng is the most calcium-dense food in the Singapore hawker diet, providing approximately 1,300mg calcium per 100g (130% of the daily AI) and 390mg per standard 30g serving (39% AI) — making it a critical dairy-free calcium source for lactose-intolerant populations. It is also exceptionally high in protein (48g/100g), vitamin B12 (14mcg/100g, 583% AI), niacin (12mg/100g), selenium (52mcg/100g), and potassium (1,200mg/100g). Vitamin D content (10mcg/100g) supports calcium absorption and insulin sensitivity. The primary clinical concern is very high sodium (2,400mg/100g; 720mg per 30g serving) and very high purine content (~900mg/100g), making it contraindicated for patients with active gout or hyperuricaemia and requiring careful portion control in hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load is negligible (GL ~0.5 per 30g), making it safe for Type 2 Diabetes management as a condiment. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fatty acid breakdown and moisture content are not available in the HPB Singapore database for this preparation; water_g and MUFA/PUFA set to -1.

ikan bilis goreng protein per serving

meal_context: ikan bilis as calcium source dairy-free

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and key micronutrients (calcium, sodium, protein, vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, phosphorus, potassium) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database — the primary approved source for Singapore hawker foods — with cross-reference to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values supplemented from USDA SR Legacy at confidence 0.9. GI is an analogous estimate (no direct measurement exists) based on macronutrient profile consistent with Sydney GI Database methodology for high-protein low-carbohydrate seafood. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not available in the HPB database for this preparation and are set to -1.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for deep-fried dried anchovy (Stolephorus spp.) is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy anchovy entries as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for fried dried anchovy is not reported in the HPB Singapore database; the deep-frying process significantly reduces moisture but an exact value is not available from approved sources. trans_fat_g confirmed zero per HPB source — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional deep-frying preparation. sugar_g confirmed zero per HPB source — no added sugars; this record covers plain fried ikan bilis, not sambal ikan bilis. dietary_fibre_g confirmed zero — whole dried fish contains no dietary fibre. vitamin_c_mg confirmed zero — heat-sensitive vitamin C is absent in dried and fried fish. Omega-3 fatty acid content (~2.8g/100g) is noted in clinical context but not broken out as a separate per_100g field as MUFA/PUFA split is unavailable. Purine content (~900mg/100g) is clinically documented in the health_context section. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for fried dried anchovy exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Copper (0.12mg/100g), manganese (0.18mg/100g), and vitamin K (1.5mcg/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy at confidence 0.9 as these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB database for this food. No discrepancies found.
